title: 2. Personal Conflict → The protagonist clashes with others, usual
content:
2. Personal Conflict → The protagonist clashes with others, usually a mentor, rival, or love interest.
3. External Conflict → The world itself creates obstacles (society, institutions, expectations).

🔥 How Whiplash Embodies This Principle
• Internal Conflict → Andrew struggles between wanting greatness and questioning if it’s worth the cost.
• Personal Conflict → Fletcher is both his mentor and greatest antagonist, constantly humiliating and manipulating him.
• External Conflict → The conservatory is an unforgiving machine that rewards only perfection, creating relentless pressure.

📌 McKee’s Principle Applied:
• Stories don’t work without struggle—characters must fight against themselves, others, or the world around them.
• Whiplash never allows Andrew a moment of ease—his growth is always tied to pain.

3. Character is Defined by Decisions Under Pressure

“True character is revealed in the choices a human being makes under pressure—the greater the pressure, the deeper the revelation.”
context: From document: 1.1.1. | The Story Problem | subheading — chunk 3 (chunk 3/8)
